coredata.dc:title: Interfacing water soluble nanomaterials with fluorescence chemosensing: Graphene quantum dot to detect Hg2+ in 100% aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chakraborti, Himadri
Sinha, Sougata
Ghosh, Subrata
Pal, Suman Kalyan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have successfully been utilized as efficient nano-sized fluorescence chemosensor to detect selectively Hg2+ in 100% aqueous solution (pH 7). The selectivity and sensitivity of the probe have been investigated by employing a number of spectroscopic techniques. The probe follows a ‘turn-off’ mechanism while sensing the highly toxic pollutant Hg2+. Steady state and time-resolved spectroscopic studies ensure that the adsorption of Hg2+ onto the surface of GQDs leads to the change in electronic structure of the probe which ultimately results in the quenching of fluorescence of GQDs. The capability of GQDs to detect Hg2+ under physiological conditions makes them interesting and useful sensing devices for biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Sensors
Spectroscopy
Turn-off
Fluorescence lifetime

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and emission spectra of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in aqueous solution.

Fluorescence spectra (λex~360 nm) of GQDs in the presence of various metal ions: Li+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ (concentration ∼60μM) in HEPES buffered aqueous solution (pH 7). Inset: Fluorescence emission titration of GQDs in the presence of Hg2+.
Absorption spectra of GQDs in HEPES buffered aqueous solution (pH 7) in the presence of various concentration of Hg2+.

Fluorescence decay of GQDs in the absence (black rectangle) and presence (red triangle) of Hg2+ (λex~375 nm). Solid lines are biexponential fitting results. Green points represent instrument response function.

head.ce:title: Interfacing water soluble nanomaterials with fluorescence chemosensing: Graphene quantum dot to detect Hg2+ in 100% aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Mandi 175001, Himachal Pradesh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have successfully been utilized as efficient nano-sized fluorescence chemosensor to detect selectively Hg2+ in 100% aqueous solution (pH 7). The selectivity and sensitivity of the probe have been investigated by employing a number of spectroscopic techniques. The probe follows a ‘turn-off’ mechanism while sensing the highly toxic pollutant Hg2+. Steady state and time-resolved spectroscopic studies ensure that the adsorption of Hg2+ onto the surface of GQDs leads to the change in electronic structure of the probe which ultimately results in the quenching of fluorescence of GQDs. The capability of GQDs to detect Hg2+ under physiological conditions makes them interesting and useful sensing devices for biological applications.

► Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as efficient sensor for highly toxic mercury ions. ► The probe works in aqueous solution at physiological conditions. ► Detection process follows fluorescence ‘turn-off’ mechanism. ► Fluorescence quenching is due to the perturbation in the electronic structure of GQD as a result of adsorption of Hg2+ onto its surface.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute and chronic toxicity of mercury and its derivatives are well documented in the literature and mercury is considered to be one among the strong pollutants as the inhalation or accumulation of mercury may create some serious health problem by attacking the central nervous system, DNA, etc. [1–4]. Therefore, selective and sensitive detection of mercury present in various samples is an important area of research in recent years. In this regard, fluorescence chemosensing is gaining utmost importance as an efficient sensing tool to detect selectively various analytes including metal ions, biomolecules, chemical warfare agents and pesticides [5–14]. Though several fluorescent probes have been developed over the years for selective detection of Hg2+ [15–22], and to the best of our knowledge, probes with capability of sensing Hg2+ in 100% water are scarcely reported [23–28].

Graphene quantum dot (GQD), one of the nifty carbon-based nanomaterial, has drawn much attention because of its unique properties like low toxicity, high fluorescence activity and relatively high photostability [29]. In this letter, we demonstrate efficient detection of mercury in 100% aqueous solution at the physiological pH using only GQDs for the first time. The probe follows a ‘turn-off’ mechanism through quenching of the fluorescence of GQDs by Hg2+ and is found to be highly selective and sensitive towards mercury.

GQDs were prepared by tailoring the carbonization degree of citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solution as reported elsewhere [30]. Similar absorption and fluorescence spectra as Ref. [30] assured the successful synthesis of GQDs ( Fig. 1). The average size of the GQDs was found to be 10.5nm (Fig. S1) which is in good agreement with previous reports. Furthermore, measured zeta potential (−23.5mV) confirmed negatively charged character of the GQD surface. GQDs exhibited a prominent absorption band having maximum at 360nm and a glowing blue fluorescence at 460nm. The emission of GQDs was independent of excitation wavelength which infers the uniformity of the sp2 matrix contained in it (Fig. S2) [30].

The emission of GQDs was quenched almost completely in the presence of Hg2+, whereas negligibly small quenching was observed in the presence of other metal ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+) ( Fig. 2). To confirm that the quenching was due to Hg2+ and not due to the anion associated with it, various salts of Hg2+ containing different types of anions were used. All Hg2+ salts showed similar quenching effect proving that Hg2+ is solely responsible for quenching (Fig. S3). To prove that the quenching was not due to a combination of Hg2+ and its counter anion, the similar experiments were carried out using various salts (of similar anions) of Na+. Interestingly, none of the Na+ salts showed quenching of fluorescence of GQDs indicating that anions have actually no effect (Fig. S3).

Fluorescence emission titration experiment was performed with the gradual addition of Hg2+ and almost complete quenching was observed upon the addition of 60μM Hg2+ (inset of Fig. 2). The limit of detection was calculated following the literature method [19] and found to be 3.36μM (Fig. S4). A competitive experiment was performed to investigate the selectivity of the probe (GQD) towards Hg2+ in the presence of various metal ions. The selectivity and sensitivity of the probe towards Hg2+ are not affected to any extent in the presence of excess amount of other metal ions except very little interference of Fe3+ (Fig. S5).

To understand the origin of the interaction between GQDs and Hg2+ absorption titration was performed. Upon the gradual addition of Hg2+, the intensity of the main absorption band (∼360nm) of GQDs was decreased and red shifted with a concomitant increase in absorption at ∼310nm ( Fig. 3). The observed change and the presence of isosbestic point in the absorption spectra infer the formation of the GQD–Hg2+ complex from free GQDs. Upward curvature in the Stern–Volmer plot (Fig. S6) indicates mixed (both static and dynamic) nature of the interaction providing further support to our prediction on complex formation in the ground state. Though the carboxylic (−COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) functionalities at the edges of GQDs could be the possible binding sites for Hg2+, the absence of any significant changes in FT-IR spectra of Hg2+–GQD complex in comparison to the FT-IR spectra of GQDs (Fig. S7) rules out these possibilities.

Since the fluorescence property of GQDs is greatly affected by their surface states [31–34], the change in fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of Hg2+ could be attributed to surface modification as a result of adsorption of Hg2+. We further confirmed this by plotting adsorption isotherms (Fig. S8). Best fit of experimental data by Freundlich model indicates multilayer adsorption of Hg2+ on GQD surface.

Fluorescence decays of GQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ were measured after excitation at a wavelength of 375nm for better understanding of quenching mechanism ( Fig. 4). The decays were well fitted using biexponential functions having a short (τ 1) and a long (τ 2) lifetime components. In the presence of Hg2+, τ 1 was decreased from 0.36ns to 0.26ns, while τ 2 remained almost same (∼1.45ns). According to a recent report [35], the fast component comes from band to band transition whereas the slow component is associated with the edge states of GQDs. Negligible effect of Hg2+ on τ 2 again discards the binding possibility of Hg2+ at the edges of GQDs. On the other hand, adsorption of mercury ions on GQD surface may influence its electronic structure resulting quenching of the lifetime component τ 1.

Finally, to reconfirm the recognition of Hg2+ by GQDs reversible binding experiment was performed in the presence of strong chelating agent EDTA. While the presence of Hg2+ quenched the fluorescence of GQDs, addition of EDTA to the same solution resulted in recovery of the fluorescence. This happened due to the formation of strong Hg2+–EDTA complex upon the addition of EDTA which actually made Hg2+ inaccessible to GQDs and GQDs remained free from Hg2+ (Fig. S9). These results clearly elucidate the sensitive recognition of Hg2+ by GQDs.

To conclude, GQD is found to be an efficient chemosensing device for detecting selectively Hg2+ in 100% aqueous solution (pH 7). After performing several experiments it is confirmed that the adsorption of Hg2+ on the surface of GQDs is the sole reason for quenching of fluorescence property of GQDs. We believe that the easy synthesis of GQDs from commercially available cheap materials, and their low toxic nature, high sensitivity and selectivity will make them valuable chemosensing materials for all sorts of biological and analytical studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was performed with the help of financial support from Indian Institute of Technology Mandi (Grant nos. IITM/SG/SUG/004 and IITM/SG/SUG/010). HC and SS are grateful to IIT Mandi for their fellowship. We sincerely acknowledge Prof. T.A. Gonsalves, Director, IIT Mandi for providing research facilities. Thanks to Dr. S. Mukhopadhyay and Mr. Dominic Narang of IISER Mohali for their help with the time-resolved fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2013.01.094.
